title: Aortic Regurgitation
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.title: Primary Valve Disease as a Cause of Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
items.content: Aortic regurgitation can result from primary valve disease, where rheumatic changes prevent proper cusp function. Congenital bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is significant, with ~20% needing surgery between 10 and 40 years of age. Other causes include membranous subaortic stenosis and prolapse of an aortic cusp.
keyPoints: Rheumatic disease leads to thickening and deformity of valve cusps.
~20% of patients with BAV require surgery between ages 10-40.
Prolapse of an aortic cusp occurs in ~15% of ventricular septal defect cases.

items.title: Primary Aortic Root Disease as a Cause of AR
items.content: Aortic regurgitation can also result from primary aortic root disease, characterized by marked dilation of the aortic annulus. Conditions such as Marfan's syndrome, idiopathic dilation, and severe hypertension contribute to this dilation. Syphilis and ankylosing spondylitis may cause cellular infiltration leading to dilation and AR.
keyPoints: Aortic root disease involves dilation without primary valve leaflet involvement.
Conditions like Marfan's syndrome can lead to aortic annulus widening.
Syphilis and ankylosing spondylitis may cause thoracic aorta scarring.

items.title: Increased LV Stroke Volume and Hemodynamic Compensation in AR
items.content: In aortic regurgitation (AR), the left ventricle's total stroke volume is increased due to both effective forward flow and regurgitant volume. Severe AR can lead to equal volumes of forward and regurgitant flows, with hemodynamic compensation primarily through increased LV end-diastolic volume. This adaptation allows for larger stroke volumes without additional myofibril shortening.
keyPoints: Total LV stroke volume is the sum of effective forward stroke volume and regurgitant volume.
In severe AR, regurgitant flow may equal forward stroke volume.
Hemodynamic compensation involves increased LV end-diastolic volume (preload).
LV dilation and eccentric hypertrophy allow larger stroke volumes without increased myofibril shortening.
Severe AR can occur with normal effective forward stroke volume and LVEF, but elevated LV end-diastolic pressure and volume.

items.title: Pathophysiological Changes Leading to Myocardial Ischemia in AR
items.content: Myocardial ischemia in AR is due to increased oxygen demands from LV dilation, hypertrophy, and elevated systolic tension, coupled with reduced coronary perfusion. This occurs because a significant portion of coronary blood flow happens during diastole when aortic pressure is low, reducing the driving pressure for coronary perfusion.
keyPoints: Myocardial ischemia can occur due to increased oxygen demands from LV dilation and hypertrophy.
Coronary blood flow primarily occurs during diastole, leading to reduced perfusion in AR.
Increased systolic tension and elevated aortic pressure contribute to higher myocardial oxygen requirements.
Reduced coronary driving pressure exacerbates ischemia risk, especially for the subendocardium.

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Acute Severe Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
items.content: In acute severe AR, such as from infective endocarditis or trauma, the left ventricle cannot dilate adequately to maintain stroke volume. This results in rapid increases in left ventricular diastolic pressure and elevated left atrial and pulmonary artery wedge pressures, potentially leading to pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock.
keyPoints: Acute severe AR can lead to rapid rise in LV diastolic pressure.
Associated with marked elevations of LA and PA wedge pressures.
Can result in pulmonary edema and/or cardiogenic shock.

items.title: Symptom Progression in Chronic Severe Aortic Regurgitation
items.content: Chronic severe AR may remain asymptomatic for 10–15 years. Early symptoms include palpitations and awareness of heartbeat, especially when lying down. Exertional dyspnea is the first symptom indicating diminished cardiac reserve, followed by orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and diaphoresis. Anginal pain may occur even without coronary artery disease.
keyPoints: Chronic severe AR can be asymptomatic for 10–15 years.
Early symptoms: palpitations, awareness of heartbeat on lying down.
Exertional dyspnea is the first symptom of diminished cardiac reserve.
Followed by orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and diaphoresis.
Anginal pain can occur without CAD.

items.title: Characteristic Physical Signs in Chronic Severe Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
items.content: In chronic severe AR, specific physical signs such as a 'water-hammer' pulse and capillary pulsations are indicative of the condition. These signs result from rapid changes in arterial pressure during systole and diastole.
keyPoints: Water-hammer pulse (Corrigan's pulse) - rapidly rising pulse collapsing suddenly due to rapid fall in arterial pressure during late systole and diastole.
Capillary pulsations (Quincke's pulse) - alternate flushing and paling of skin at the root of the nail with applied pressure.
Booming 'pistol-shot' sound over femoral arteries (Traube's sign).
To-and-fro murmur when femoral artery is compressed (Duroziez's sign).

items.title: Pulse Pressure and Palpation in Chronic Severe AR
items.content: Chronic severe AR leads to widened arterial pulse pressure due to systolic hypertension and lowered diastolic pressure. Palpation reveals specific characteristics of the left ventricular impulse and thrills.
keyPoints: Widened arterial pulse pressure from systolic hypertension and lower diastolic pressure.
LV impulse is heaving, displaced laterally and inferiorly with prominent systolic expansion and diastolic retraction.
Diastolic thrill palpable along the left sternal border in thin-chested individuals.
Systolic thrill palpable in suprasternal notch, transmitted upward along carotid arteries.

items.title: Auscultation Features of Severe AR
items.content: In severe AR, auscultation reveals specific sounds and murmurs that help in diagnosis. The absence of the aortic valve closure sound and presence of diastolic murmurs are key indicators.
keyPoints: Absence of aortic valve closure sound ($ \\$Delta_r $).
High-pitched, blowing, decrescendo diastolic murmur heard best in third intercostal space along left sternal border.
Murmur becomes louder and longer with increased severity, becoming holodiastolic.
Austin Flint murmur: soft, low-pitched, rumbling mid-to-late diastolic murmur not associated with mitral valve obstruction.

items.title: ECG Findings in Chronic Severe Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
items.content: In chronic severe AR, ECG often shows signs of left ventricular hypertrophy and LV strain. This includes ST-segment depression and T-wave inversion in specific leads, along with possible left axis deviation or QRS prolongation.
keyPoints: ST-segment depression and T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6
Left axis deviation and/or QRS prolongation may be present

items.title: Echocardiographic Criteria for Severe Aortic Regurgitation
items.content: Severe AR is characterized by specific echocardiographic findings such as a central jet width exceeding 65% of the LV outflow tract, regurgitant volume ≥60 mL/beat, and regurgitant fraction ≥50%. Additionally, diastolic flow reversal in the descending thoracic aorta is noted.
keyPoints: Central jet width > 65% of LV outflow tract
Regurgitant volume ≥ 60 mL/beat
Regurgitant fraction ≥ 50%
Diastolic flow reversal in proximal descending thoracic aorta

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGATION
items.title: Urgent Surgical Intervention in Acute Severe AR
items.content: In cases of acute severe aortic regurgitation, intravenous diuretics and vasodilators like sodium nitroprusside may offer temporary stabilization. However, surgery is urgently indicated as the definitive treatment, typically within 24 hours of diagnosis.
keyPoints: Stabilization with IV diuretics and vasodilators (e.g., sodium nitroprusside) is usually short-lived
Intra-aortic balloon counterpulsation is contraindicated
Beta blockers are best avoided to prevent further reduction in cardiac output or slowing of heart rate
Surgery is the treatment of choice and should be performed within 24 hours
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGATION
items.title: Management Strategies for Chronic AR
items.content: In chronic aortic regurgitation, surgery is indicated upon the onset of symptoms or left ventricular systolic dysfunction. Medical management with diuretics and vasodilators can be used temporarily. Vasodilators are not useful to extend the compensated phase in asymptomatic patients but should manage hypertension (systolic BP >140 mmHg). Beta blockers and ARBs like losartan may help retard aortic root enlargement in young patients with Marfan's syndrome.
keyPoints: Surgery is indicated upon symptom onset or LV systolic dysfunction
Diuretics and vasodilators (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers) can be used temporarily
Vasodilators do not extend the compensated phase in asymptomatic patients but manage hypertension (systolic BP >140 mmHg)
Beta blockers and losartan may retard aortic root enlargement in Marfan's syndrome
Avoid isometric exercises in severe AR with associated aortopathy
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.title: Timing of Surgical Intervention in Chronic Severe Aortic Regurgitation
items.content: Surgical intervention for chronic severe aortic regurgitation (AR) should be timed to coincide with the onset of left ventricular (LV) dysfunction but before symptoms develop. Delaying surgery beyond 1 year from symptom onset or LV dysfunction can result in failure to restore normal LV size and function.
keyPoints: Surgery is optimal when performed at the onset of LV dysfunction, prior to symptom development.
Delaying surgery >1 year from symptom onset or LV dysfunction often results in suboptimal restoration of LV size/function.
Clinical follow-up with echocardiography every 6-12 months is necessary for timely surgical intervention.

items.title: Indications and Thresholds for Aortic Valve Replacement in Severe AR
items.content: Aortic valve replacement (AVR) is indicated for symptomatic patients with severe AR regardless of LV function. In asymptomatic patients, AVR should be considered if there's progressive LV dysfunction defined by specific echocardiographic criteria.
keyPoints: AVR is necessary in symptomatic patients irrespective of LV function.
Asymptomatic patients with LVEF <55%, LV end-systolic dimension >50 mm (>25 mm/m²), or LV diastolic dimension >65 mm should consider AVR.
Smaller dimensions may be thresholds for smaller individuals or those with progressively worsening LV function/size.

items.title: Management of Acute Severe Aortic Regurgitation
items.content: Acute severe aortic regurgitation requires prompt surgical intervention within 24–48 hours, as it can be lifesaving.
keyPoints: Surgical treatment is required urgently (within 24-48 hours) for acute severe AR.
Prompt surgery in acute cases may be lifesaving.

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with the development of aortic regurgitation (AR) due to primary valve disease?
items.explanation: Rheumatic disease results in changes that prevent proper opening and closure of the aortic valve, leading to AR.

items.question: What is a common consequence of membranous subaortic stenosis related to aortic regurgitation?
options: Thickening and scarring of the aortic valve leaflets
items.explanation: Membranous subaortic stenosis results in a high-velocity systolic jet that often leads to thickening and scarring of the aortic valve leaflets, causing secondary AR.

items.question: Which condition is associated with marked aortic annular dilation leading to aortic regurgitation without primary involvement of the valve leaflets?
options: Medial degeneration of the ascending aorta
items.explanation: Medial degeneration of the ascending aorta, often associated with Marfan's syndrome, can lead to marked aortic annular dilation and AR.

items.question: In patients with severe aortic regurgitation (AR), what happens to the effective forward stroke volume?
options: It remains normal or slightly reduced at rest.
It increases due to increased preload.
It equals the volume of regurgitant flow.
items.explanation: In patients with severe AR, the effective forward stroke volume usually remains normal or only slightly reduced at rest.

items.question: Which law explains why left ventricular (LV) dilation increases systolic tension in chronic aortic regurgitation?
items.explanation: LV dilation increases the systolic tension required to develop any given level of systolic pressure through Laplace's law.

items.question: What is a typical sign of left ventricular (LV) dysfunction in chronic severe AR?
items.explanation: An early sign of LV dysfunction is a reduction in the EF.

items.question: Which group is more likely to have primary valvular aortic regurgitation with associated rheumatic mitral valve disease?
items.explanation: Women predominate among patients with primary valvular AR who have associated rheumatic mitral valve disease.

items.question: What may precipitate or seriously aggravate preexisting symptoms in patients with rheumatic or congenital aortic valve involvement?
items.explanation: A history compatible with infective endocarditis may sometimes be elicited from patients with rheumatic or congenital involvement of the aortic valve.

items.question: What is an early complaint in chronic severe aortic regurgitation?
items.explanation: Uncomfortable awareness of the heartbeat, especially on lying down, may be an early complaint in chronic severe AR.

items.question: Which physical sign is characterized by the alternate flushing and paling of the skin at the root of the nail when pressure is applied to the tip?
items.explanation: Quincke's pulse is characterized by alternate flushing and paling of the skin at the root of the nail when pressure is applied to the tip.

items.question: What auscultatory feature suggests that aortic regurgitation (AR) is caused by aneurysmal dilation of the aortic root?
options: A murmur louder along the left sternal border
A mid-systolic ejection murmur at the base of the heart
A diastolic murmur louder along the right sternal border
items.explanation: A diastolic murmur that is louder along the right sternal border suggests AR caused by aneurysmal dilation of the aortic root.

items.question: Which pulse characteristic is associated with chronic severe aortic regurgitation?
options: A rapidly rising 'water-hammer' pulse that collapses suddenly
A consistently high diastolic pressure pulse
items.explanation: Chronic severe aortic regurgitation is associated with a rapidly rising 'water-hammer' pulse that collapses suddenly.

items.question: What auscultatory finding indicates the absence of aortic valve closure sound in patients with severe AR?
options: An absent aortic valve closure sound ($\Delta_r$)
items.explanation: In patients with severe AR, the aortic valve closure sound ($\Delta_r$) is usually absent.

items.question: Which echocardiographic finding is characteristic of chronic severe aortic regurgitation (AR) due to the impact of the regurgitant jet?
options: Diastolic fluttering of the anterior mitral leaflet
Increased central jet width on color flow Doppler imaging
Rapid deceleration time in continuous-wave Doppler profile
Decrease in left ventricular end-systolic dimension
items.explanation: Diastolic fluttering of the anterior mitral leaflet is a characteristic finding due to the impact of the regurgitant jet.

items.question: What does a rapid deceleration time in the continuous-wave Doppler profile indicate in patients with acute severe AR?
options: Rapid increase in left ventricular diastolic pressure
items.explanation: A rapid deceleration time indicates a rapid increase in left ventricular diastolic pressure.

items.question: Which imaging modality can be used to assess increased LV interstitial and replacement fibrosis in AR?
items.explanation: CMR imaging can assess increased LV interstitial and replacement fibrosis using late gadolinium enhancement.

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.question: Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in patients with acute severe aortic regurgitation?
items.explanation: Intra-aortic balloon counterpulsation is contraindicated in acute severe aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.question: In chronic aortic regurgitation, which of the following is an indication for surgery?
options: Asymptomatic patients with systolic blood pressure >140 mmHg
Use of vasodilators to extend the compensated phase
Onset of symptoms or left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction
items.explanation: The onset of symptoms, or LV systolic dysfunction, is an indication for surgery in chronic aortic regurgitation.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.question: Which medication may be useful to retard the rate of aortic root enlargement in young patients with Marfan's syndrome and aortic root dilation?
items.explanation: Both beta blockers and the ARB losartan may be useful to retard the rate of aortic root enlargement in young patients with Marfan's syndrome.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.question: In patients with chronic severe aortic regurgitation (AR), what is the recommended interval for noninvasive testing with echocardiography to determine the optimal timing of surgery?
items.explanation: Echocardiography should be performed at ~6- to 12-month intervals for optimal timing of surgery.

items.question: What is the indication for aortic valve replacement (AVR) in patients with severe AR?
options: In asymptomatic patients regardless of LV function
In symptomatic patients irrespective of LV function
items.explanation: AVR is indicated for the treatment of severe AR in symptomatic patients irrespective of LV function.

items.question: What is a contraindication to performing the Ross procedure in patients with pure aortic regurgitation?
options: Presence of aortic annular dilation and/or an associated aortopathy
items.explanation: The presence of aortic annular dilation and/or an associated aortopathy would eliminate the option for the Ross procedure.

items.question: What is the overall operative mortality rate for isolated aortic valve replacement (AVR) performed for pure AR?
items.explanation: The overall operative mortality rate for isolated AVR performed for pure AR is ~1–2%.

items.statement: Aortic regurgitation due to primary valvular disease is most commonly caused by infective endocarditis.
items.explanation: Rheumatic disease, not infective endocarditis, is more commonly associated with AR due to primary valve disease.

items.statement: Aortic regurgitation can result from aortic root diseases such as Marfan's syndrome.
items.explanation: Medial degeneration of the ascending aorta, which may be associated with Marfan's syndrome, can lead to AR.

items.statement: In chronic severe AR, equilibration between aortic and left ventricular pressures may occur toward the end of diastole.
items.explanation: Equilibration between aortic and LV pressures may occur toward the end of diastole in patients with chronic severe AR.

items.statement: In acute severe AR, left ventricular compliance is increased.
items.explanation: In acute severe AR, the LV is unprepared for the regurgitant volume load and its compliance is normal or reduced.

items.statement: Pulmonary edema and/or cardiogenic shock can develop rapidly in acute severe aortic regurgitation.
items.explanation: In patients with acute severe AR, pulmonary edema and/or cardiogenic shock may develop rapidly.

items.statement: Systemic fluid accumulation is an early symptom in chronic severe aortic regurgitation.
items.explanation: Systemic fluid accumulation, including congestive hepatomegaly and ankle edema, may develop late in the course of the disease.

items.statement: The arterial pulse pressure in chronic severe AR is narrowed due to systolic hypertension.
items.explanation: In chronic severe AR, the arterial pulse pressure is widened, not narrowed.

items.statement: The Austin Flint murmur indicates hemodynamically significant mitral valve obstruction in patients with severe AR.
items.explanation: The Austin Flint murmur is not associated with hemodynamically significant mitral valve obstruction.

items.statement: In chronic severe AR, the apex is displaced upward in the frontal projection on a chest X-ray.
items.explanation: The apex is displaced downward and to the left in the frontal projection.

items.statement: CMR imaging can provide detailed assessment of LV volumes, aortic size, and contour.
items.explanation: CMR imaging allows for accurate assessment of these parameters.

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.statement: Beta blockers are recommended for acute severe aortic regurgitation to reduce heart rate and allow more time for diastolic filling.
items.explanation: Beta blockers are best avoided in acute severe AR as they may reduce cardiac output further or slow the heart rate, worsening diastolic filling.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.statement: Patients with severe aortic regurgitation should avoid isometric exercises.
items.explanation: Patients with severe AR, particularly those with an associated aortopathy, are advised to avoid isometric exercises.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.statement: Trans-catheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is recommended for patients with severe AR who are surgical candidates.
items.explanation: TAVI is not recommended for patients with severe AR who are surgical candidates.

items.statement: Patients with acute severe aortic regurgitation require prompt (24–48 h) surgical treatment.
items.explanation: Prompt surgical treatment within 24–48 hours is necessary for patients with acute severe AR.

items.assertion: Anginal chest pain can occur in patients with severe aortic regurgitation even in the absence of coronary artery disease.
items.reason: Nocturnal angina may be particularly troublesome and accompanied by marked diaphoresis, often not responding to sublingual nitroglycerin.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why anginal chest pain can occur without coronary artery disease.

items.assertion: A booming 'pistol-shot' sound over the femoral arteries indicates Traube's sign.
items.reason: Traube's sign is characterized by a booming 'pistol-shot' sound heard over the femoral arteries in severe AR.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: In acute severe AR, a wide pulse pressure is typically observed.
items.reason: Acute severe AR may be accompanied by only a slight widening of the pulse pressure due to elevated LV end-diastolic pressure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because acute severe AR does not typically show a wide pulse pressure; the reason is true.

items.subtopic: ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION
items.assertion: Beta blockers can sometimes provide incremental blood pressure lowering in patients with chronic aortic regurgitation and hypertension.
items.reason: They can also lessen the sense of forceful heart action that many patients find uncomfortable.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Aortic Regurgitation · ACUTE AORTIC REGURGITATION (FIG. 273-2) | CHRONIC AORTIC REGURGITATION

items.assertion: Surgical repair may be possible when a leaflet has been perforated during infective endocarditis or torn from its attachments by thoracic trauma.
items.reason: Primary surgical repair is rarely feasible in such cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided is false; primary surgical repair may be possible in these situations.

items.assertion: Operative and late mortality risks are largely dependent on the stage of the disease and myocardial function at the time of operation.
items.reason: The overall operative mortality rate for isolated AVR performed for pure AR is ~5–10%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion; the operative mortality rate for isolated AVR in pure AR is actually ~1-2%.
